claim: When picking AI startup ideas, look for jobs people are already paying other people to do—these are guaranteed pain points with proven market demand and pricing established
actionable: Survey your target market to identify expensive labor categories (customer support, paralegal work, insurance adjustment) and build AI tools to automate or assist with those specific workflows
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: TAM for AI applications is 1000x larger than traditional SaaS because the addressable market shifts from per-seat pricing to the total salary cost of professionals doing that job
actionable: Calculate TAM by summing all salaries paid for your target job category rather than number of potential users times monthly SaaS price
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: AI applications fall into three categories: assist (help professionals do their job better), replace (fully automate the job), or do the unthinkable (enable previously economically infeasible work like reviewing millions of documents)
actionable: Classify your AI product into one of these three categories and ensure your entire value proposition aligns with that category—mixing categories dilutes focus
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: To build reliable AI applications, first understand exactly what a world-class professional would do step-by-step when solving the problem, then translate those steps into prompts or code
actionable: Interview domain experts or work in the field yourself to document the exact sequence of steps an expert takes, then build LLM prompts and code that implement those steps
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Product quality matters far more for marketing and sales in AI applications than most VCs claim—building a genuinely excellent product creates word-of-mouth and press, which are free marketing
actionable: Prioritize product excellence over traditional marketing spend in your go-to-market strategy, and measure success through word-of-mouth referral rate and unsolicited press mentions
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Most AI systems fail at production reliability not because the model is weak but because builders skip evaluation—passing 60% of evals and assuming 'AI just can't do this task' instead of iterating
actionable: Create 100+ test cases per prompt and 100+ tests for overall workflows before shipping to beta; expect to spend 2+ weeks iterating on a single prompt to improve from 60% to 97% eval pass rate
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Evaluation frameworks should be objective and measurable (e.g., true/false outputs or 0-7 scales) rather than subjective judgments to enable reliable iteration
actionable: Structure your evals to output binary or numeric ratings whenever possible; use tools like promptfoo to systematically test against holdout sets while iterating
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: AI fails in predictable patterns once you start intensive prompt tuning—watch for categories of errors and add specific instructions to guide the model away from those errors
actionable: Log failure patterns as you iterate; for each recurring error type, add explicit negative instructions (e.g., 'do NOT do X') and examples to your prompt
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Never stop iterating on prompts; new models require re-tuning and a single word addition can move accuracy up 1%, which is extremely valuable in regulated domains like law, medicine, and finance
actionable: Create a daily practice of running evals against new models and submitting GitHub PRs to update prompts—treat prompts as living code, not static configurations
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Pricing AI services should be based on the value delivered (what customers would pay a human professional) not on token consumption or seat licenses
actionable: Interview customers on their willingness to pay and preferred payment model (flat fees preferred by many for predictability); charge 10-20% of the value you're displacing from human labor
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Building trust with enterprise customers on AI requires visible head-to-head comparisons and pilots where they run your AI solution alongside existing human or incumbent processes side-by-side
actionable: Structure pilots to run your AI and the incumbent process in parallel, measuring speed and quality metrics that allow customers to directly compare outputs
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Pilot revenue is not real revenue—many AI startups report inflated ARR from 6-month pilots that never convert to production contracts, risking a 'mass extinction event' when pilots expire
actionable: Track pilot-to-production conversion rates separately from ARR; assume pilots require active onboarding, training, and deployment engineer support to have any chance of converting
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Product extends beyond UI pixels—customer success, training, support interactions, deployment engineer presence, and thoughtful onboarding are core product components that determine whether customers actually use and renew
actionable: Hire deployment engineers or customer success specialists early; treat them as essential to product, not overhead; measure customer activation and usage adoption in parallel with feature releases
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

claim: Building defensibility against competitors is achieved through extensive prompt tuning, data integrations, and fine-grained workflow logic that competitors cannot easily replicate, not through model ownership
actionable: Do not fear that foundation models are commodities—your moat is the months of iterative tuning, customer-specific data integrations, and specialized workflows that take competitors years to rebuild
source_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups
